mission.title: Install and Configure Open WebUI
mission.description: Open WebUI is a self-hosted AI platform that provides a user-friendly interface for various LLM runners like Ollama and OpenAI-compatible APIs. It is designed for offline operation and offers extensibility through plugins, making it suitable for both personal and enterprise use. Key trade-offs include the need for self-hosting and management of infrastructure.

steps.title: Install the CLI
steps.description:
Install the Open WebUI CLI to facilitate the deployment process.

```bash

curl -LO https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui/releases/download/v0.8.9/open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/open-webui

```

> This command downloads the CLI binary, makes it executable, and moves it to your PATH.
commands: curl -LO https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui/releases/download/v0.8.9/open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/open-webui

steps.title: Create a Kubernetes Cluster
steps.description:
Set up a new Kubernetes cluster with the specified version and node count.

```bash

gcloud container clusters create open-webui-cluster --cluster-version=1.30 --num-nodes=3 --region=us-central1

```

> This command creates a GKE cluster with 3 nodes in the specified region.

steps.title: Set Up Kubeconfig
steps.description:
Configure your kubeconfig to interact with the newly created cluster.

```bash

gcloud container clusters get-credentials open-webui-cluster --region=us-central1

```

> This command updates your kubeconfig file with the cluster's credentials.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
Check the status of the nodes in your cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> This command lists all nodes in the cluster along with their status.

steps.title: Install Open WebUI
steps.description:
Deploy Open WebUI using Helm with the specified version.

```bash

helm repo add open-webui https://charts.openwebui.com && helm repo update

helm install open-webui open-webui/open-webui --version 0.8.9 --namespace open-webui --create-namespace

```

> Add the Open WebUI Helm repository.
> Install Open WebUI in the specified namespace.
commands: helm repo add open-webui https://charts.openwebui.com && helm repo update

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Configure autoscaling and monitoring for your Open WebUI deployment.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-webui/open-webui/main/deploy/autoscale.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-webui/open-webui/main/deploy/monitoring.yaml

```

> Apply autoscaling configuration.
> Apply monitoring configuration.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Open WebUI
uninstall.description:
Remove Open WebUI and its associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall open-webui --namespace open-webui

kubectl delete namespace open-webui

```

> Uninstall the Open WebUI release.
> Delete the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Open WebUI
upgrade.description:
Backup your current deployment, upgrade to the latest version, and verify the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get all -n open-webui -o yaml > open-webui-backup.yaml

helm upgrade open-webui open-webui/open-webui --version 0.8.9 --namespace open-webui

kubectl get pods -n open-webui

```

> Backup current deployment.
> Upgrade Open WebUI to the latest version.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get all -n open-webui -o yaml > open-webui-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the kubeconfig is not set up correctly or the cluster is not running.

**Fix:**
Run 'gcloud container clusters get-credentials open-webui-cluster --region=us-central1' to update your kubeconfig.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to locate the specified Helm chart'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the Helm repository is not added or updated.

**Fix:**
Run 'helm repo add open-webui https://charts.openwebui.com && helm repo update' to add and update the repository.

troubleshooting.title: Pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate an issue with the application configuration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n open-webui' to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources allocated for the deployment.

**Fix:**
Increase the node size or number of nodes in your cluster.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Ollama models, improved API integration features.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see output similar to 'NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE' when running 'kubectl get pods -n open-webui'. All pods should be in the 'Running' state.
codeSnippets: curl -LO https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui/releases/download/v0.8.9/open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv open-webui-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/open-webui
helm repo add open-webui https://charts.openwebui.com && helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the necessary tools and resources before starting the installation.
